articles.title: Shakira and Burna Boy Drop 'Dai Dai' With Messi and Mbappé
articles.summary: Shakira and Burna Boy released the official music video for 'Dai Dai', the FIFA 2026 World Cup anthem, on May 23, packed with cameos from Lionel Messi, Kylian Mbappé, Erling Haaland, and a roster of the world's biggest football stars. The four-minute visual was directed by Hannah Lux Davis and shot in Miami, opening with Messi, Mbappé, and Haaland declaring 'We are ready' on the pitch. It is Shakira's second official World Cup song, following 'Waka Waka (This Time for Africa)' from 2010.
articles.content:
Shakira has done this before, and she knows exactly how to do it. On Saturday May 23, she and Burna Boy dropped the official music video for 'Dai Dai', the anthem of the 2026 FIFA World Cup, and it is a full-on global event packed into four minutes.

The video opens with Lionel Messi, Kylian Mbappé, and Erling Haaland walking onto the pitch and declaring 'We are ready' for Shakira. That is quite the opening act. Directed by Hannah Lux Davis and shot in Miami, the visual takes Shakira from the top of Mexico City's Angel of Independence monument to a desert landscape dancing alongside a group of African children, before Burna Boy appears for his verse while Shakira stands atop a glowing globe against a starry sky. It is big, theatrical, and utterly committed to the moment.

The star power does not stop with Messi, Mbappé, and Haaland. The full cameo list includes Luis Díaz, Vinícius Jr., Rodri, Takefusa Kubo, Santiago Giménez, Alphonso Davies, Jamal Musiala, Christian Pulisic, Harry Kane, and Bukayo Saka. The video ends with archival clips from past World Cups as Shakira shouts out legends including Diego Maradona, Paolo Maldini, Romário, Cristiano Ronaldo, David Beckham, Kaká, and Messi again for good measure. Countries namechecked include Brazil, Argentina, Colombia, the US, and the Netherlands.

For context, 'Dai Dai' marks the second time Shakira has been the face of an official FIFA World Cup song. The first was 'Waka Waka (This Time for Africa)', the anthem for the 2010 World Cup in South Africa, which became one of the best-selling singles of that entire year and remains one of the most streamed World Cup songs ever. The pairing with Burna Boy, one of Afrobeats' biggest global names, gives 'Dai Dai' a continental reach that mirrors the original song's African soul.

The 2026 FIFA World Cup kicks off on June 11 at Estadio Azteca in Mexico City and wraps up on July 19 at MetLife Stadium in New Jersey. Shakira is also set to co-headline the halftime show at that final alongside Madonna and BTS, in a live event produced by Global Citizen and curated by Chris Martin of Coldplay. Safe to say she is having a summer.
keyPoints: Shakira and Burna Boy released the 'Dai Dai' music video on May 23, 2026
Lionel Messi, Kylian Mbappé, and Erling Haaland open the video declaring 'We are ready'
The four-minute video was directed by Hannah Lux Davis and shot in Miami
'Dai Dai' is Shakira's second official FIFA World Cup anthem after 'Waka Waka' in 2010
Shakira will also co-headline the World Cup final halftime show on July 19 with Madonna and BTS
articles.wikiSearchQuery: Shakira Burna Boy Dai Dai FIFA World Cup 2026

articles.title: Mohamed Salah Leaves Liverpool: 257 Goals and a Culture Changed Forever
articles.summary: Mohamed Salah played his final game for Liverpool on Sunday, May 24, ending a nine-year spell at Anfield that produced 257 goals, two Premier League titles, a Champions League, and a legacy that went far beyond football. The 33-year-old Egyptian became a cultural icon for Muslim fans worldwide, redefining what representation could look like at the top of the sport. Former manager Jurgen Klopp, Liverpool legend Steven Gerrard, all-time top scorer Ian Rush, and others paid tribute to one of the club's greatest ever players.
articles.content:
When Mohamed Salah arrived at Liverpool from Roma in 2017, there was cautious optimism. He had already failed to break through at Chelsea and was considered a decent attacking option, nothing more. What followed over the next nine years was, by any measure, one of the greatest individual runs in Premier League history.

The numbers alone are staggering. In 438 appearances for Liverpool, Salah scored 257 goals, making him the club's third-highest scorer ever behind only Ian Rush and Roger Hunt. His 193 Premier League goals make him the top foreign goalscorer in the history of the league. He won four Golden Boots, was named PFA Player of the Year and FWA Player of the Year three times each, and lifted every major trophy going: the Premier League, Champions League, FA Cup, League Cup, UEFA Super Cup, and FIFA Club World Cup. He also holds the record for the most goal involvements for a single club in Premier League history.

The farewell was not without pain. Salah's final season at Liverpool was fractured by a very public falling out with manager Arne Slot, who dropped him from the starting lineup late in 2025. Salah told interviewers he felt the club had "thrown him under the bus" and that "someone doesn't want me in the club." Relations never fully recovered, and the season ended without a trophy. He leaves on a free transfer after Liverpool agreed to cut short the final year of his contract extension.

But the legacy stretches well beyond trophies and statistics. For Muslim fans across Britain and around the world, Salah's visibility at the very summit of world football was genuinely transformative. His trademark sujud celebration, bowing down in Islamic prostration after goals, was seen on pitches and screens across the globe every week. As journalist Ahmed Shooble put it: "If you told me at 10-years-old that the best player in the league is Muslim, I'd have called you a liar." Salah was authentically himself at all times, and that mattered enormously to communities who had rarely seen their identity reflected at that level.

Jurgen Klopp, the manager who built Liverpool's modern dynasty around Salah, once said after a particularly stunning goal against Manchester City: "Only the best players in the world score goals like this." Steven Gerrard, Ian Rush, and other Anfield legends have joined the chorus of praise this week as Salah's Anfield chapter closes. What comes next is unclear. At 33 he is expected to seek elite European football rather than a move to the Saudi Pro League, with his eyes still on Champions League goals and more trophies. But whatever happens next, Mohamed Salah has already written his name permanently into Liverpool's history, and into the broader story of the sport.
keyPoints: Mohamed Salah ends his Liverpool career with 257 goals and 438 appearances across nine seasons
He is the top foreign goalscorer in Premier League history with 193 league goals
Salah won every major club trophy at Liverpool including the Champions League and two Premier League titles
His final season was marred by a public falling out with manager Arne Slot, and he leaves on a free transfer
Beyond football, Salah's legacy as a proud, visible Muslim at the top of world sport changed what representation looks like for millions of fans

articles.title: Cristian Mungiu Wins Second Palme d'Or at Cannes for Fjord
articles.summary: Romanian director Cristian Mungiu took the top prize at the 79th Cannes Film Festival for Fjord, a culture-war drama starring Sebastian Stan and Renate Reinsve. This is Mungiu's second Palme d'Or, making him one of only ten filmmakers in history to win it twice. The win also gave distributor Neon its seventh consecutive Palme d'Or, a streak that has no precedent in the festival's history.
articles.content:
Cristian Mungiu just made Cannes history. Again. The Romanian director won the Palme d'Or at the 79th Cannes Film Festival on Saturday night for Fjord, becoming one of only ten filmmakers ever to win the festival's top prize twice. His first Palme came 19 years ago for 4 Months, 3 Weeks and 2 Days, the shattering 2007 abortion drama that put Romanian cinema on the world map.

Fjord is a very different kind of film but tackles themes just as raw. It follows a Romanian family of Evangelical Christians who move to Norway and end up in a child abuse case after the Norwegian social care system intervenes over their parenting. The film stars Sebastian Stan and Renate Reinsve, both of whom joined Mungiu on stage to collect the award. It is a co-production between Romania, Norway, Sweden, Denmark, Finland and France, and it picked up the François Chalais Prize, the FIPRESCI Prize and the Ecumenical Jury Prize on top of the Palme.

The film divided critics during the festival. Some read it as a genuine exploration of the tension between religious conservatism and social liberalism. Others were uneasy about where its sympathies lay. Mungiu addressed that directly earlier in the week, saying: "Expressing doubts about our liberal society doesn't mean for a second that I'm a defender of a conservative society." The jury, headed by South Korean filmmaker Park Chan-wook, clearly found the debate itself to be the point. Park joked at the post-ceremony press conference that he had not wanted to give the Palme to any of the films, because it is an award he has never won himself.

The win is also a remarkable business story. Distributor Neon has now taken the Palme d'Or for seven consecutive years, a run that is simply without compare in the modern festival landscape. The ceremony itself was described by many as comparatively low-key for Cannes. Barbra Streisand was absent to receive her honorary Palme d'Or after cancelling her in-person appearance due to recovery from a knee injury. And for the first time in years, no mainstream Hollywood blockbuster premiered on the Croisette, with only two US films in the main competition.

Other big winners on the night included Andrey Zvyagintsev's Minotaur, which took the Grand Prix, and a shared Best Director prize going to Javier Calvo and Javier Ambrossi for The Black Ball alongside Pawel Pawlikowski for Fatherland. Mungiu was measured as ever accepting the award, saying: "All awards are contextual." For a filmmaker who has now been rewarded by Cannes at three separate festivals, the context is looking very good indeed.
keyPoints: Cristian Mungiu won his second Palme d'Or for Fjord, 19 years after his first for 4 Months, 3 Weeks and 2 Days
Fjord stars Sebastian Stan and Renate Reinsve and explores religious conservatism vs. social liberalism in Norway
The film also won the François Chalais Prize, FIPRESCI Prize and Ecumenical Jury Prize
Neon extended its extraordinary run to seven consecutive Palme d'Or wins
The 79th Cannes festival was notably quieter, with no Hollywood blockbusters in competition

articles.title: Oli McBurnie's Late Strike Sends Hull City to the Premier League
articles.summary: Oli McBurnie scored a stoppage-time winner at Wembley on Saturday, May 23, as Hull City beat Middlesbrough 1-0 to secure promotion to the Premier League. The final came after one of the most chaotic weeks in English football history, with Southampton expelled from the playoffs entirely over a spying scandal. Hull are heading back to the top flight, and the drama that got them there is almost impossible to believe.
articles.content:
Oli McBurnie wrote his name into Hull City folklore with a late, late goal at Wembley that sent the Tigers back to the Premier League. The striker found the net in stoppage time to seal a 1-0 win over Middlesbrough in the Championship play-off final on Saturday, May 23. It was the kind of gut-punch finish that dreams are made of, and the Hull fans inside Wembley absolutely lost their minds.

But to understand just how wild this moment was, you need to know the week that came before it. Southampton had originally beaten Middlesbrough in the play-off semi-finals and were set to face Hull in the final. Then everything exploded. A Southampton first-team analyst intern was spotted hiding near Middlesbrough's training ground on May 7, apparently filming a session just days before the first leg of their semi-final. Middlesbrough complained to the English Football League. The EFL charged Southampton, and then the full truth came out: Southampton admitted to spying on not just Middlesbrough, but also Oxford United in December 2025 and Ipswich Town in April 2026.

The punishment was brutal and historic. Southampton were expelled from the play-offs entirely and handed a four-point deduction for next season. They appealed the decision, but an independent League Arbitration Panel dismissed the appeal on Wednesday, May 20, upholding the original sanctions. Southampton themselves called it "the largest penalty ever imposed on an English football club," arguing it could cost them around £200 million in lost Premier League revenue. The EFL did not budge. Middlesbrough were reinstated in their place, found out on Tuesday evening they had a Wembley final to prepare for, and had just days to get ready.

So when the final kicked off, both teams were carrying a week's worth of chaos on their shoulders. The heat at Wembley drained the players further, and the match itself was tight and tense rather than a classic. But then McBurnie struck in stoppage time and none of that mattered anymore. Hull City manager Sergej Jakirovic had his side ready when it counted. The Tigers, who had knocked out Millwall in the semi-finals with a 2-0 aggregate win thanks to goals from Mohamed Belloumi and Joe Gelhardt, were simply the last team standing after the most extraordinary play-off saga English football has ever seen. The Premier League awaits.
keyPoints: Oli McBurnie scored a stoppage-time winner as Hull City beat Middlesbrough 1-0 in the Championship play-off final at Wembley.
Southampton were expelled from the play-offs after admitting to spying on Middlesbrough, Oxford United, and Ipswich Town during the 2025-26 season.
Southampton's appeal was dismissed by an independent panel on May 20, with their four-point deduction for next season also upheld.
The play-off final is often called the richest single game in sport, with promotion to the Premier League worth at least £200 million to the winning club.
Hull City manager Sergej Jakirovic's side had beaten Millwall in the semi-finals before facing a last-minute change of opponent.

articles.title: Demi Moore, Tilda Swinton, and Guillermo del Toro Clash Over AI at Cannes
articles.summary: The 79th Cannes Film Festival became the film world's biggest battleground over AI, with major stars and directors taking sharply opposing sides. Demi Moore sparked a social media firestorm by calling resistance to AI a "battle we will lose," while Tilda Swinton fired back that AI "doesn't have a chance" against original cinema. Guillermo del Toro cut to the heart of the debate, arguing that nobody is even defining AI correctly — and that generative AI, specifically, is the real threat.
articles.content:
The 79th Cannes Film Festival had plenty of glamour and great films. But the conversation that kept pulling everyone back was about artificial intelligence. Panels, beach parties, late-night terrace talks — AI was everywhere on the Croisette, and for once, the film world was not running from it.

The spark came early. Demi Moore, serving as a juror on the nine-member feature film jury led by Park Chan-wook, stood at the opening press conference and said what a lot of people in the industry have quietly been thinking. "AI is here," she told reporters. "And so to fight it is to fight something that is a battle that we will lose." The reaction on social media was swift and brutal, with critics accusing Moore of selling out the creative community. But throughout the festival, the evidence backed her up: films at the Cannes market were openly advertising their AI use as a selling point, not hiding it as a dirty secret.

Not everyone agreed to wave the white flag. Tilda Swinton gave a masterclass that became one of the week's most quoted moments. She argued that AI is only a threat when filmmakers get lazy and formulaic. "I believe as long as what we're not producing is formulaic and in some way tiring for the audience, AI doesn't have a chance," Swinton said. Her remedy? Make films that are "messy, adventurous" — the kind of human experiences that no algorithm can replicate. Guillermo del Toro, in Cannes to present a 4K restoration of Pan's Labyrinth marking its 20th anniversary, gave the debate its sharpest framing: "We have to start defining AI, because they're trying to pass five types of things under one single name." He drew a hard line between useful tools like tracking software and generative AI, which he said removes the artist from the equation entirely.

Meanwhile, the market side of Cannes told its own story. Animated family film Critterz billed itself as "human-led but AI-assisted." Doug Liman's $70 million Bitcoin movie had buyers lining in and out of the Majestic Hotel, powered by technology from Ryan Kavanaugh's company Acme AI. One veteran sales agent summed up the shift bluntly: "A year ago, some people were using AI, but they were embarrassed to admit it. This year, they aren't even hiding it." Cannes Film Festival director Thierry Frémaux tried to hold the middle ground, saying the festival stands with artists and workers whose jobs are at risk, but acknowledging that legislation and controls are needed. The 79th Cannes did not settle the AI debate. But it made one thing clear: the question is no longer whether AI will reshape cinema. It already is.
keyPoints: Demi Moore told the Cannes jury press conference that fighting AI is "a battle we will lose," sparking a social media backlash
Tilda Swinton countered at her masterclass that AI has no chance against original, adventurous filmmaking
Guillermo del Toro argued the whole debate is muddled because people lump every type of AI together
Films at the Cannes market openly used AI as a selling point, a first for a major festival
Cannes director Thierry Frémaux called for legislation to protect workers threatened by AI

articles.title: Usyk Stops Verhoeven With One Second Left in Round 11
articles.summary: Oleksandr Usyk retained his world heavyweight titles by stopping Rico Verhoeven at 2:59 of the 11th round in Giza, Egypt — with just one second left on the clock. The late stoppage has sparked fierce debate, as Verhoeven was widely seen as ahead on the scorecards going into that round. Referee Mark Lyson's decision to end the fight has split the boxing world and put the result under a cloud of controversy.
articles.content:
Oleksandr Usyk kept his unbeaten record intact on Saturday night, but the way it happened is going to be talked about for a long time. Usyk stopped Rico Verhoeven at exactly 2:59 of the 11th round at the Pyramids of Giza in Egypt, with just one second remaining before the bell. That single second is now the most debated moment in boxing.

For ten rounds, Verhoeven gave Usyk serious problems. The Dutch kickboxing legend — widely considered one of the greatest kickboxers of all time — used his size and unusual hunched-over style to keep Usyk off balance. By the end of Round 8, all three judges had the fight scored 76-76, a stunning number given Usyk entered as a massive minus-2500 favourite. Verhoeven landed hard right hands in Rounds 7, 8 and 9 and had the crowd and the commentary team believing an all-time upset was brewing.

Then came the 11th. Usyk landed a vicious right uppercut that dropped Verhoeven late in the round. Verhoeven got back to his feet, unsteady but still defending himself in the corner. Referee Mark Lyson stepped in and waved it off. The problem? There was one second left. Verhoeven had not been stopped cleanly and was still fighting back when Lyson ended it. The stoppage immediately blew up on social media and on the DAZN broadcast, with critics — including Jake Paul — ripping the decision.

Usyk moves to 25-0 with 16 knockouts and retains his WBC heavyweight title. His WBA and IBF belts were not on the line in this bout but stay with him regardless. Usyk said in his post-fight interview he was open to an immediate rematch, but the WBC is expected to order him to face Agit Kabayel next. Verhoeven, at 1-1 in boxing, proved he belongs at the highest level. Most people who watched feel he earned a chance to hear that final bell.
keyPoints: Usyk stopped Verhoeven at 2:59 of Round 11, with just one second left before the bell
Referee Mark Lyson's stoppage was immediately controversial — Verhoeven was still defending himself
Verhoeven was widely believed to be ahead on all three judges' scorecards going into Round 11
Usyk improves to 25-0 and retains his WBC heavyweight title
A rematch is already being floated, though the WBC may order Usyk to fight Agit Kabayel next

articles.title: GigaAI's SeeLight S1 Is Moving Into 100 Wuhan Homes Right Now
articles.summary: Chinese tech firm GigaAI unveiled the SeeLight S1 on May 21, 2026 — a two-armed, wheeled humanoid robot it describes as China's first general-purpose household robot — and is already deploying a pilot fleet of 100 units into hi-tech employee housing in Wuhan this month. The robot can chop vegetables, fry eggs, do laundry, make beds, and open curtains, with GigaAI aiming to bring its hardware cost down to under 100,000 yuan (about $14,700) by June 2027. This is the most ambitious real-world test of a home humanoid robot anywhere on the planet right now.
articles.content:
The Jetsons fantasy just got a lot more real. Chinese robotics company GigaAI launched the SeeLight S1 on May 21, 2026, calling it China's first general-purpose household humanoid robot. And unlike most robotics announcements that live forever in demo videos, GigaAI is actually putting 100 units into real homes in Wuhan this month.

The SeeLight S1 was developed in collaboration with the Hubei Humanoid Robot Innovation Centre and the Hubei Humanoid Robotics Industry Alliance. It uses a wheeled base instead of legs, paired with two robotic arms built to handle the messy reality of actual home life. The task list is genuinely impressive: chopping vegetables, frying eggs, loading laundry, making beds, and opening curtains. GigaAI calls the underlying technology an advanced embodied AI system, designed to navigate and plan in unpredictable environments where furniture moves, floors are cluttered, and no two kitchens look the same.

The first 100 SeeLight S1 units are heading into housing used by hi-tech industry employees in Wuhan, with GigaAI CEO Zhu Zheng saying the company plans to offer the robots free of charge to selected families in the city during the first half of 2027. Priority in that rollout may go to households with elderly family members, children, or pets. Zhu also set a bold price target: bringing the SeeLight S1's hardware cost below 100,000 yuan, roughly $14,700, by June 2027. That is about half what the robot currently costs to build.

The why behind China pushing this so hard matters. China faces what demographers call a 4-2-1 problem: single children increasingly responsible for supporting two parents and four grandparents as the population ages rapidly. Household robots are being positioned as a practical answer to that pressure, not just a cool gadget. GigaAI is not alone in this space. In the US, startup Gatsby made headlines on May 14 when it sent a humanoid robot to clean a consumer's home in San Francisco for $150, the first such service visit in US history. But GigaAI's SeeLight S1 pilot is the larger and more structured deployment, with a real commercial price target and a government-backed testing program.

The honest caveat here is that surviving 100 different homes, with 100 different layouts, cooking habits, and unpredictable pets, is a very different challenge from a polished demo. The data GigaAI collects from this Wuhan pilot will determine whether the SeeLight S1 becomes a genuine mass-market product or an expensive lesson in how hard it is to build a robot that lives with you. If it works, the addressable market shifts from tech enthusiasts to nearly everyone.
keyPoints: GigaAI launched the SeeLight S1 on May 21 — China's first general-purpose household humanoid robot
100 units are being deployed into hi-tech employee housing in Wuhan this month as a real-world pilot
The robot can chop vegetables, fry eggs, do laundry, make beds, and open curtains using two robotic arms and a wheeled base
GigaAI CEO Zhu Zheng is targeting a hardware price below $14,700 by June 2027, roughly half the current cost
China's aging population and 4-2-1 demographic problem are a major driver behind the push for home robotics

articles.title: Amish Businessmen in Ohio Are Using ChatGPT at Work
articles.summary: Men in Holmes County, Ohio — home to the largest Amish community in the United States — are quietly using ChatGPT to write emails, draft contracts, and run their businesses. It is not a wholesale embrace of Silicon Valley culture but a careful, practical choice that fits neatly inside the Amish tradition of evaluating technology on its own merits. One business owner even used ChatGPT to write his wife a Valentine's Day note, and she noticed immediately.
articles.content:
Here is a sentence that would have seemed absurd a few years ago: Amish business owners in rural Ohio are using ChatGPT. Not secretly, not reluctantly. Practically and openly, because it helps them get work done.

According to a New York Magazine investigation reported on by Futurism, the men of Holmes County, Ohio — the area with the highest concentration of Amish people in the entire country — have been adopting ChatGPT as a genuine work tool. Ian Wengerd, an Amish father of six who runs a metal-fabrication company with around 30 employees, explained it simply. "The more I used it, the more I thought this could actually be a good thing," he told New York Magazine. His company handles state work, federal work, and private contracts, and without tools like ChatGPT, he says he and his team would be out of a job. For families running manufacturing, construction, and agriculture businesses, the AI chatbot is less of a cultural statement and more of a practical necessity.

But do not mistake this for a tech free-for-all. Historian Marcus Yoder estimates that fewer than half of Holmes County's Amish population is online at all, and of those, under 10 percent have ever tried an AI tool. Not a single person interviewed by New York Magazine uses a smartphone. Most rely on flip phones or stripped-down "dumb phones," and their internet access is filtered through Christian censoring services and limited almost entirely to work. Ian's 19-year-old cousin John Wengerd, a chicken salesman and property manager, put it in a way that honestly sounds healthier than most of our digital habits: "I can't lay in bed for half an hour asking Chat stuff. So the times when I'm vulnerable it's not at my fingertips."

The community's approach to new technology has always been more nuanced than the stereotype of total rejection. There are some 2,600 Amish churches across the United States, and each congregation makes its own independent decisions about what tools members can use. The rule of thumb is whether a technology supports work without reshaping family life, spirituality, or community structure. ChatGPT, used on a work phone during business hours, fits that test for some. Daniel Wengerd, a business owner and minister, was clear that the community is not abandoning its values: "We're not just opening the door to anything." That said, Daniel did use ChatGPT to write his wife Mary Ellen a Valentine's Day note — and she saw straight through it. "Reading this, I was like, This is so not my husband," she told New York Magazine, laughing. The Amish, it turns out, may have figured out a healthier relationship with AI than the rest of us.
keyPoints: Amish businessmen in Holmes County, Ohio are using ChatGPT to write emails, draft contracts, and manage family-run companies
Ian Wengerd, who runs a 30-person metal-fabrication firm, says ChatGPT has become essential to keeping his business running
Historian Marcus Yoder estimates fewer than half of Holmes County's Amish population is online, and under 10 percent have used AI
No one interviewed uses a smartphone — internet access is limited to work and filtered through Christian censoring services
Amish congregations evaluate each technology individually; ChatGPT passes because it supports work without disrupting home or community life

articles.title: My Hero Academia Wins Anime of the Year at Crunchyroll's 10th Awards
articles.summary: My Hero Academia FINAL SEASON took home Anime of the Year at the 2026 Crunchyroll Anime Awards, held in Tokyo on May 23. The milestone 10th edition of the ceremony drew a record 73 million fan votes from around the world. The Weeknd, a longtime anime fan, personally presented the top award.
articles.content:
The 2026 Crunchyroll Anime Awards wrapped up in Tokyo on Saturday, and anime fans now have their definitive winners. The 10th edition of the ceremony, held at the Grand Prince Hotel Shin Takanawa, was a full celebration of how far anime has come as a global cultural force.

My Hero Academia FINAL SEASON walked away with the night's biggest prize: Anime of the Year. Director Kenji Nagasaki accepted the award, which was presented by none other than The Weeknd, a well-known anime fan who showed up to mark the occasion in style. My Hero Academia (also known as Boku no Hero Academia) is the beloved superhero series that has run for a decade, following a boy born without powers in a world full of superheroes. Winning on its final season makes the victory feel especially emotional for long-time fans.

Demon Slayer: Kimetsu no Yaiba Infinity Castle dominated the film side of the evening, winning Film of the Year. Composers Yuki Kajiura and Go Shiina took home Best Score for their work on that same film. The Best Anime Song award went to Kenshi Yonezu for "IRIS OUT" from Chainsaw Man: The Movie: Reze Arc. Other major category winners included Lazarus for Best Original Anime, One Piece for Best Continuing Series, and Gachiakuta for Best New Series. Director Tatsuya Nagamine, known for his work on Ojamajo Doremi, Dragon Ball Super, and One Piece, received the Global Impact Award.

The night was packed with performances that felt like a love letter to anime history. Japanese singer Yoko Takahashi performed a 30th anniversary tribute to Neon Genesis Evangelion with the iconic theme "A Cruel Angel's Thesis." Rock band ASIAN KUNG-FU GENERATION played "Haruka Kanata," the beloved second opening theme for Naruto. PORNOGRAFFITTI performed in honor of My Hero Academia's own 10th anniversary. Presenters included RZA of Wu-Tang Clan, Puerto Rican rapper and singer Young Miko, and K-pop stars BamBam and TEN. The full ceremony will be available to stream on Crunchyroll and Crunchyroll's YouTube channel.
keyPoints: My Hero Academia FINAL SEASON won Anime of the Year, presented by The Weeknd
Demon Slayer: Kimetsu no Yaiba Infinity Castle won Film of the Year
Kenshi Yonezu won Best Anime Song for 'IRIS OUT' from Chainsaw Man: The Movie: Reze Arc
A record 73 million fan votes were cast globally for the 10th annual ceremony
Lazarus, One Piece, and Gachiakuta also took home major awards

articles.title: Waymo Pulls Robotaxis Off All US Freeways Over Flood Failures
articles.summary: Waymo has suspended highway rides in every city it operates after its robotaxis repeatedly struggled to handle flooded roads and construction zones. The ban affects Los Angeles, San Francisco, Miami, and Phoenix, while Atlanta service was also separately paused after an unoccupied vehicle stalled in floodwater. The move exposes a deeper problem: a software recall pushed to Waymo's entire 3,791-vehicle fleet weeks earlier failed to stop the incidents.
articles.content:
Waymo just hit a major speed bump. Waymo has put the brakes on its robotaxi access to highways to update software that helps the driverless cars navigate flooded roads and construction zones, halting freeway service in all four cities where it is available: Los Angeles, San Francisco, Miami, and Phoenix. Waymo continues to offer rides on streets and all other thoroughfares in those metropolitan areas.

The freeway ban did not come out of nowhere. The highway ban came one day after Waymo suspended service throughout Atlanta, where it operates in conjunction with Uber, after an unoccupied robotaxi stalled in floodwater on Wednesday. In Atlanta, a Waymo robotaxi carrying a journalist drove into a flooded street and became stranded for nearly an hour before being removed. The freeway suspension was also tied to a separate alarming incident: a video posted to X showed a Waymo robotaxi driving through a freeway construction zone at highway speeds before police gave chase.

What makes this especially awkward is that Waymo thought it had already fixed the flooding problem. Waymo suspended robotaxi service across five cities on May 21, 2026, after a software patch it pushed to its entire 3,791-vehicle fleet less than two weeks earlier failed to prevent another autonomous vehicle from driving into standing water. The original trigger was an April 20 incident in San Antonio where a robotaxi encountered a flooded section of road on a 40 mph corridor, slowed but did not stop, and was swept into Salado Creek. The car was unoccupied and recovered days later. Waymo has acknowledged it still has no permanent fix for the problem.

The timing is rough for a company with enormous ambitions. Waymo raised $16 billion in February 2026 at a $126 billion valuation and has set a goal of offering 1 million paid rides per week by the end of 2026, roughly four times its current rate of approximately 250,000 rides per week. Waymo is also currently testing its new Zeekr-built robotaxi, which it calls Ojai, and is expected to start offering rides in that vehicle in the coming months. A Waymo spokesperson said the company has "temporarily paused freeway operations" as it works to integrate recent technical learnings into its software, and expects to resume these routes soon.
keyPoints: Waymo suspended all freeway rides in Los Angeles, San Francisco, Miami, and Phoenix on May 21, 2026
Atlanta service was also paused separately after a robotaxi stalled in floodwater for about an hour
A software patch pushed to all 3,791 Waymo vehicles weeks earlier failed to prevent new flooding incidents
Waymo still has no permanent fix for its flood-navigation problem
The disruption threatens Waymo's goal of 1 million paid rides per week by end of 2026

articles.title: Meng Xiaoyi's PettiChat Claims to Translate Your Pet at 95% Accuracy
articles.summary: Chinese startup Meng Xiaoyi has launched PettiChat, an AI-powered collar that claims to translate your cat or dog's sounds, movements, and emotions into human speech with nearly 95% accuracy. The device has already racked up 10,000 preorders since going on sale in May 2026, priced at 799 yuan (about $118). Critics, however, are calling it a "human intelligence test" given that the company is just months old and has released zero independent scientific evidence.
articles.content:
Everyone who has ever owned a dog or cat has wondered the same thing: what on earth are they actually trying to say? Hangzhou-based startup Meng Xiaoyi thinks it has the answer, and it comes in the form of a collar called PettiChat.

PettiChat sits around your pet's neck and uses built-in microphones, motion sensors, and accelerometers to capture sounds, movements, and behavioral patterns in real time. According to Meng Xiaoyi, the device can identify more than 20 emotional expressions and convert them into human-readable phrases on your phone within 1.2 seconds. Demo videos show a cat's meow translating to "I wanna play" and a dog's bark becoming "I'm hungry." The company says PettiChat also works in reverse, letting owners speak commands that get converted into pet-friendly audio signals the animal can respond to.

Under the hood, PettiChat runs on Alibaba Cloud's Qwen large language model, trained on millions of pet voiceprint data points matched to behavioral and environmental patterns. Meng Xiaoyi claims that combination pushes accuracy to close to 95%. The preorder response has been striking: since opening orders on May 1, the company has confirmed over 10,000 units reserved, and Meng Xiaoyi has secured $1 million in seed funding on the back of the buzz.

But not everyone is convinced. Critics on Chinese social media have been blunt, with some calling PettiChat a "human intelligence test" rather than a genuine pet translator. The skepticism has a solid foundation: Meng Xiaoyi was only founded in January 2026, and the company has not published any independent, peer-reviewed studies or transparent datasets to back up that headline 95% figure. Pet translator apps have existed for years, usually built purely for laughs with no real science behind them. Whether PettiChat is a genuine breakthrough or just a very well-funded novelty, 10,000 pet owners are about to find out when the device officially launches on May 30.
keyPoints: Meng Xiaoyi's PettiChat collar claims to translate pet sounds and behavior into human speech with nearly 95% accuracy
The device is powered by Alibaba Cloud's Qwen AI model and processes translations in about 1.2 seconds
Over 10,000 units were preordered since May 1, priced at 799 yuan (~$118)
Meng Xiaoyi raised $1 million in seed funding despite being founded only in January 2026
No independent scientific studies have been published to validate the accuracy claims

articles.title: Record Club Wants to Be the Letterboxd That Music Nerds Need
articles.summary: Record Club is making a serious push to become the go-to social cataloging app for music fans, filling a gap that Letterboxd fills for film lovers and Goodreads fills for book readers. The app lets users rate, review, log listens, and share custom lists in a clean modern interface that leaves cluttered rival Rate Your Music in the dust. It pulls its entire music database from the open-source encyclopedia MusicBrainz, which means it can compete on community and design rather than spending millions building catalog data from scratch.
articles.content:
If you love movies, you have Letterboxd. If you love books, you have Goodreads. But if you love music? Until now, the options have been pretty rough. Record Club wants to fix that.

There has never been a solid equivalent to Goodreads or Letterboxd for music lovers, and while Rate Your Music exists, its interface is crowded and it feels more geared toward longer-form reviews than casually logging your listening habits and connecting with other fans. Record Club is going after exactly that underserved crowd.

Record Club is clean and modern, with a streamlined interface that is quite similar to Letterboxd. The features are exactly what you would hope for. You can rate and review records or mark them as listened to, see what your friends are listening to, see what albums are trending with other users, list your five favorite albums plus five records in heavy rotation, and create custom ranked or unranked lists to share with others. That last one is great for tracking your top albums of the year or building genre deep-dive guides.

You can also follow your favorite artists as well as entire record labels, making it easy to stay on top of new releases from labels like 4AD, AD 93, Fire Talk, and Warp. And here is the smart technical move underneath all of it: Record Club pulls all of its data from the open-source music encyclopedia MusicBrainz. By leaning on that infrastructure, Record Club avoids one of the most expensive problems in music software, which is building and maintaining a clean catalog from scratch, so the app is competing on presentation and community rather than on raw data ownership.

The Letterboxd comparison works because it points to a very specific kind of cultural product: one that makes cataloging social and gives everyday users a shared vocabulary for taste. Music has long had places to store metadata and places to argue about records, but far fewer places that make listening feel social by default. Record Club's own Year in Review for 2025 is another sign that it wants to become a taste engine, not just a logging tool. The gap in the market is real and Record Club is stepping into it with confidence.
keyPoints: Record Club is a social music app for rating, reviewing, logging, and list-making — think Letterboxd but for albums
Rival Rate Your Music has a massive catalog but a cluttered interface not built for casual social sharing
Record Club pulls its music data from the open-source MusicBrainz encyclopedia, avoiding the cost of building catalog infrastructure
Users can follow artists and entire record labels to track new releases easily
Record Club's 2025 Year in Review signals its ambition to be a taste-discovery engine, not just a database

articles.title: Messi and Mbappé Front Monopoly's Wild New Panini World Cup Mashup
articles.summary: Hasbro has unveiled the Monopoly Panini Prizm: FIFA World Cup Game, a board game that replaces properties and railroads with real Panini Prizm soccer cards featuring stars like Lionel Messi, Kylian Mbappé, Pelé, and Christian Pulisic. It is part of a broader FIFA World Cup 2026 game collection that also includes Monopoly Deal FIFA World Cup 2026 and Connect 4 Shots FIFA World Cup 2026. The timing is no accident — the tournament kicks off June 11, and this drop is already on shelves at Amazon, Target, and Walmart.
articles.content:
The 2026 FIFA World Cup merchandise machine is fully in motion. Hasbro just dropped a collection of officially licensed FIFA World Cup 2026 games, and the centerpiece is something genuinely new: the Monopoly Panini Prizm: FIFA World Cup Game, a board game that fuses classic Monopoly gameplay with real collectible Panini Prizm soccer cards.

Forget buying Boardwalk or building hotels. In this version, players build their squads using Panini Prizm cards that feature current stars and soccer legends. Instead of collecting properties, you compete to take control of historic World Cup matches. Land on a square someone else already owns? You battle for points by comparing player stats on the cards and rolling special dice. The bench replaces jail. The goal is to end up at the FIFA World Cup 2026 as the winner.

The faces on those cards are a serious lineup. Lionel Messi, Kylian Mbappé, Italian great Paolo Maldini, Brazilian legends Pelé and Marta, and Team USA stars Christian Pulisic and Mia Hamm all feature in the base set of 100 names. Each Monopoly box includes 16 cards, and collectors will want to hunt for the Gold Cracked Ice Monopoly Prizm and Hat Trick parallels, which are exclusive to the board game packs. There is also a Monopoly Panini Prizm FIFA World Cup 2026 Booster Box that adds 24 more Panini Prizm cards for anyone who wants to bulk up their squad.

This is not the first time Hasbro and Panini have teamed up. In February 2023, the two companies launched Monopoly Prizm: NBA Edition with the same collect-and-compete concept using basketball stars. The FIFA version rounds out a full Hasbro World Cup collection that also includes Monopoly Deal FIFA World Cup 2026, a fast-moving card game where the first player to collect three sets of players wins, and Connect 4 Shots FIFA World Cup 2026, where players use soccer cleat launchers to kick balls into the grid. All of it is available now ahead of the June 11 tournament kickoff.
keyPoints: Hasbro launched the Monopoly Panini Prizm: FIFA World Cup Game, blending board game play with real collectible Panini Prizm soccer cards
Players build squads using cards featuring Lionel Messi, Kylian Mbappé, Pelé, Christian Pulisic, Mia Hamm, and more
Each game box includes 16 cards, with exclusive Gold Cracked Ice and Hat Trick Prizm parallels only found in board game packs
A Booster Box adds 24 more Panini Prizm cards, and the collection also includes Monopoly Deal and Connect 4 Shots FIFA editions
Everything is available now at Amazon, Target, and Walmart ahead of the June 11 World Cup kickoff
